---
title: "Conclusion"
---
```{r conclusion-setup, include=FALSE}
library(readxl)
library(tidyverse)

r8_data <- read_excel(
  "case-and-demographic-characteristics-table-r8-2023-2024.xlsx",
  col_names = FALSE
)[-c(1, 2), ]

names(r8_data)[2]  <- "naics"
names(r8_data)[3]  <- "case_type"
names(r8_data)[4]  <- "total_rate"
names(r8_data)[10] <- "fall_rate"

mfg_total <- as.numeric(r8_data |> filter(naics == "31-33", case_type == "DAFW") |> pull(total_rate) |> first())
mfg_falls <- as.numeric(r8_data |> filter(naics == "31-33", case_type == "DAFW") |> pull(fall_rate)  |> first())
fin_total  <- as.numeric(r8_data |> filter(naics == "52",   case_type == "DAFW") |> pull(total_rate) |> first())
fin_falls  <- as.numeric(r8_data |> filter(naics == "52",   case_type == "DAFW") |> pull(fall_rate)  |> first())

fin_fall_share <- round(fin_falls / fin_total * 100, 1)
mfg_fall_share <- round(mfg_falls / mfg_total * 100, 1)
fin_fall_pct   <- round(6  / 18  * 100, 1)
mfg_fall_pct   <- round(52 / 353 * 100, 1)
```


## Summary of Findings

This report examined whether office workers face greater fall risk than manufacturing workers using two independent federal datasets. 

This report looked at the workplace injury risk across manufacturing and office sectors using both BLS nonfatal and fatal injury data. The findings confirm that manufacturing workers face much higher injury rates overall, but that falls are disproportionately common in office environments.

The nonfatal injury data showed that falls account for `r fin_fall_share`% of all injuries in finance and insurance, compared to just `r mfg_fall_share`% in manufacturing. The fatal injury data showed the same pattern: falls caused `r fin_fall_pct`% of deaths in finance and insurance versus `r mfg_fall_pct`% in manufacturing. The CFOI fatal data confirmed this pattern: falls caused `r fin_fall_pct`% of deaths in finance and insurance
versus `r mfg_fall_pct`% in manufacturing.

These results suggest clear and practical next steps: safety officers in office settings should treat fall prevention as a top priority. Even though overall injury numbers are low, when office workers do get hurt, it is most likely from a fall. Simple changes such as slip-resistant flooring, clear walkways, proper lighting, and employee awareness programs can help to reduce this risk.

There are some limitations to note. The SOII relies on employer self-reporting, which may undercount injuries in workplaces with weaker safety cultures. Broad NAICS categories also include a wide range of job types, so rates may not perfectly represent desk-based workers specifically. Median days away from work (a useful measure of injury severity) was not available in the tables used here and would strengthen future analyses.

Overall, the data shows a clear case for workplace safety,  recognizing fall risk as a significant concern even in low-injury office environments.

::: {.callout-important}
## Executive Takeaway
Office workers are not at greater *absolute* fall risk than manufacturing
workers. But falls are a disproportionately large share of the injuries office
workers do experience in both nonfatal and fatal outcomes.Fall prevention must be treated as a greater threat in office scenarios.
:::
